next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

Rhea had written the low tide time on the inside of her wrist in blunt pencil, and every few minutes she looked at it as if skin might revise itself out of spite. The digits were already smudging with sweat. In the bat lab's yellow light they looked like a second pulse under her bone. She was on her knees in front of an open equipment case, sorting cables into two piles with the tidy impatience she brought to anything that had once worked and no longer did. The room smelled of rubbing alcohol, warm plastic, and the sweet rot of old guano baked into plywood.

"You still have your boots on," she said without looking up. "That gives you a chance at being useful. Come with me."

Mina leaned in the doorway with a flashlight tucked under one arm and pretended she had not been watching the back of Rhea's neck for a full minute. Rhea's braid was pulled so tight it changed the shape of her face, all those narrow, severe lines made sharper by the heat. A crescent of white zinc clung to one ear where she had missed it that afternoon. She had changed out of her donor-safe shirt and into an old gray tank with a university logo almost scrubbed away by bleach. That should not have made her look less official. It did.

"That an invitation," Mina said, "or a conscription?"

"Field labor doesn't improve if you flirt at it." Rhea snapped a battery door closed and finally glanced up. Her mouth twitched, not quite a smile. "The west recorder died again. I want the card before the tide comes back over the route."

"West where."

"Under the old intake line."

The phrase hit Mina a ha...
open_hooks_to_carry: Rhea recruits Mina for bat-netting and lets the night slow down around work rather than seduction.
The women build trust through side detail and fieldwork competence rather than direct confession.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...als."

"Speak for yourself."

"You're the one with the government pen."

That got the shadow of his grin back. He leaned in as if to kiss her again and then thought better of it, or thought too much of it, and stepped away first. It was sensible and infuriating. She took the shell path back toward camp with the notebook still blank in her pocket and the exact turn of blue alive in her head with a precision that hurt.

The stilts creaked when she climbed them. The camp smelled of old frying oil gone cold, damp wood, bird shit on the railings, and the thin medicinal trace of bug spray everybody wore this time of year. She moved quietly past the screened walkway. Behind one door somebody snored. Behind another, a radio murmured low enough that only the rhythm got through. Ordinary life had kept going while the creek briefly opened and showed her one lit muscle of impossible history.

In the sink of the outdoor wash station she rinsed her hands and watched black grit and a faint sheen of fuel swirl toward the drain. The water never got the smell fully off. She was glad of that. The residue kept the sighting from drifting too fast into self-doubt.

When she finally lay down on her narrow camp bed, the sheet rough against her salt-stiff legs, she knew exactly how little she had. She had no photo, no sample, no witness steady enough to take into a hearing and win. She had only one blue coil under blackwater, Tomas's hands on her waist in a hidden cut, and the knowledge that the rumor had moved from local story to private fact inside her body.

It was enough to keep her from sleep.
state_deltas_required: Rhea's suspicion, Mina's need to normalize herself, and the camp's thin walls
Mina stops imagining Rhea as an obstacle and begins wanting her as a person.
